Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
South Asian Traditions

Nyāya

2nd c. CE onward

A realist school of mind centered on valid cognition, objecthood, self, and philosophical therapy.

Rather than treating these thinkers as isolated commentators, the most fruitful approach is to read them as one long Nyāya arc: Vātsyāyana gives the classical architecture; Uddyotakara hardens it against Buddhist epistemology, especially Dignāga; Udayana integrates it into a mature Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika realism and a powerful defense of selfhood; Gaṅgeśa transforms the whole inheritance into the technical idiom of Navya-Nyāya, with exceptional precision about cognition, intentionality, epistemic defeat, and apperception.

The core thesis is this: Nyāya philosophy of mind is a pramāṇa-centered realist theory of minded agency. It is not primarily a theory of “consciousness” as a private inner glow, and it is not a Cartesian “mind” opposed simply to body. It is a layered account in which the real subject is ātman, the internal organ or attentional connector is manas, cognitive episodes are jñāna/buddhi, knowledge is pramā, knowledge-sources are pramāṇa, and embodied action is driven by desire, aversion, pleasure, pain, effort, memory, and error. The result is a philosophy of mind that is simultaneously metaphysical, epistemological, semantic, psychological, moral, and soteriological. Nyāya’s own list of knowables includes self, body, senses, objects, cognition, mind, activity, defects, rebirth, experiential result, suffering, and liberation; that list itself shows that its “philosophy of mind” cannot be separated from agency and the problem of suffering.

1. Historical position: from Old Nyāya to Navya-Nyāya

The approximate chronology is as follows. The Nyāya-sūtra is usually placed around the early centuries CE; Vātsyāyana’s Nyāyabhāṣya is often dated around the fifth century; Uddyotakara’s Nyāyavārttika around the sixth; Udayana around the late tenth or eleventh; and Gaṅgeśa, author of the Tattvacintāmaṇi, in the fourteenth century or, in some chronologies, around c. 1325. This gives us more than eight centuries of development, from classical Nyāya to the highly technical “new” Nyāya.

Thinker Main work or role Philosophical-mind significance Vātsyāyana Nyāyabhāṣya, the earliest extant full commentary on the Nyāya-sūtra Establishes Nyāya as a realist, epistemological, action-guiding system; cognition is understood through its capacity to guide successful action. Uddyotakara Nyāyavārttika Defends Nyāya realism against Buddhist logicians such as Dignāga; sharpens the theory of perception, language, and inferential cognition. Udayana Ātmatattvaviveka, Nyāyakusumāñjali, other works Produces a major defense of the enduring self and of Nyāya realism against Buddhist no-self and momentariness; integrates Nyāya with Vaiśeṣika metaphysics. Gaṅgeśa Tattvacintāmaṇi Refines cognition, knowledge, intentionality, defeaters, inference, and apperception in the technical language of Navya-Nyāya.

The field-position of these figures is important. Nyāya begins as a tradition of reasoning, debate, and epistemology, but it becomes one of the most sophisticated premodern theories of mind because it treats cognition as a causal and normative event: something produced by reliable means, directed toward objects, capable of error, and embedded in practical life. The Nyāya-sūtra’s opening concern with the means of knowledge is not an accidental epistemological preface; it is the entry point into a broader account of the self, action, bondage, and liberation. Vātsyāyana glosses Nyāya as inquiry through knowledge-sources, and Uddyotakara stresses the coordinated use of those knowledge-sources in philosophical investigation.

The historical adversary most relevant for philosophy of mind is Buddhist epistemology, especially the Dignāga-Dharmakīrti line. Buddhist opponents tend toward momentariness, no enduring self, suspicion of universals, and a sharp distinction between nonconceptual perception and conceptual construction. Nyāya answers with realism about external objects, realism about universals, an enduring self, and the claim that conceptually structured perception can still be genuinely perceptual and world-directed. Uddyotakara’s project is explicitly described as re-establishing Nyāya against Dignāga, while also absorbing parts of Dignāga’s logical innovations.

Gaṅgeśa is traditionally treated as the inaugurator of Navya-Nyāya, but this should not be understood as a simple rupture. He follows Udayana closely in metaphysics and inherits the broad Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika realist framework. His originality lies less in abandoning Old Nyāya than in creating an exact analytical language for its problems: knowledge, inferential warrant, objecthood, conceptual qualification, defeaters, and the cognition of cognition.

2. Basic architecture of Nyāya philosophy of mind

A first warning is essential: manas should not be translated simply as “mind” if by “mind” one means the whole subjective field. In Nyāya, manas is an internal organ, often described as atomic, that connects the self with sensory and internal states. The subject of experience is not manas but ātman, the self. Cognitions, desires, aversions, pleasures, pains, and efforts are qualities or events belonging to the self. The body and senses are instruments; the self is the owner, experiencer, cognizer, and agent.

Term Working translation Role in the philosophy of mind ātman self, subject, soul Enduring substance that owns cognitions, desires, pleasures, pains, efforts, memories, and agency. manas internal organ, inner sense, attentional connector Mediates between self and senses or internal states; explains why ordinary cognition is serial rather than simultaneous. jñāna / buddhi / upalabdhi / pratyaya cognition, awareness episode A mental quality/event of the self; directed toward an object. pramā knowledge, veridical cognition A true cognition produced by a proper knowledge-source. pramāṇa means/source of knowledge Causal instrument of knowledge: perception, inference, comparison, testimony. viṣaya / viṣayatā object / objecthood, aboutness The intentional directedness of cognition toward what it is of or about. anuvyavasāya apperception, cognition of cognition A later cognition that takes an earlier cognition or mental event as its object. saṃskāra disposition, trace Latent imprint enabling memory, recognition, habits, and concept deployment. icchā, dveṣa, prayatna desire, aversion, effort Motivational states linking cognition to action. sukha, duḥkha pleasure, pain Affective states of the self; central to bondage and liberation.

The causal-psychological picture can be rendered schematically:

object or internal state → sense organ or manas → contact with self through manas → cognition arises in the self → possible apperception of that cognition → desire, aversion, or effort → bodily, verbal, or mental action → karmic and experiential consequences.

This model is important because it shows why Nyāya is neither reductive materialism nor pure introspectionism. The mind is not reducible to body, but cognition is not free-floating either. It is causally mediated by body, senses, objects, and manas. Mental life is embodied, causal, and world-involving, but its bearer is an enduring self distinct from the body.

3. Vātsyāyana: cognition as guide to action

Vātsyāyana’s Nyāyabhāṣya is the foundational classical commentary on the Nyāya-sūtra. It ranges over epistemology, metaphysics, philosophy of language, dialectics, and value theory, and it gives the later tradition much of its organizing vocabulary. Modern scholarship rightly emphasizes that Vātsyāyana treats cognition not as a private spectacle but as a guide to action: a cognition matters because it enables pursuit, avoidance, indifference, inquiry, correction, and eventually liberation.

For Vātsyāyana, truth is not reducible to practical success, but practical success is a major sign of truth. If I cognize something as water and drink successfully, or cognize something as fire and avoid being burned, my cognition is action-guiding because it has captured the object in a way that matters for life. This is not pragmatism in the sense that usefulness creates truth; rather, successful action is evidence that the cognition was veridical. The Nyāya tradition therefore begins philosophy of mind from a practical-epistemic relation: cognition, object, action, and success.

This gives Nyāya an anti-skeptical orientation. A cognition produced by a proper pramāṇa can be trusted unless there is a specific defeater. Doubt and error are real, but they are parasitic on generally successful cognition. The skeptic who denies all knowledge still uses perception, inference, language, and practical discrimination in making the denial. Nyāya therefore treats cognitive trust as default but defeasible, not as absolute infallibility.

Vātsyāyana also anchors the theory of mind in the self. Cognition, desire, aversion, effort, pleasure, and pain are not free-standing events. They need a locus, and that locus is the self. The classical Nyāya argument is not simply “I introspect a soul”; it is more metaphysical: mental qualities require a substance in which they inhere, and neither body, senses, nor momentary cognitions can adequately play that role.

The relation between self and manas is one of Nyāya’s most distinctive contributions. The self is not constantly manifest as conscious; cognition arises only when the appropriate causal conditions are present. Manas is required because the self must be connected with sense faculties and internal states. This explains why, in ordinary experience, we do not have all sensory cognitions simultaneously. The single internal organ channels attention and cognition serially.

Vātsyāyana’s philosophy of mind is therefore already a theory of attention, agency, and embodied cognition. It decomposes “mind” into subject, internal organ, sense faculty, cognition, affect, memory-trace, and action. It does not begin from the modern problem “How does consciousness arise from matter?” It begins from “What must be the structure of self, cognition, and world if knowledge, error, action, memory, desire, and liberation are possible?”

4. Uddyotakara: defending realist mind against Buddhist epistemology

Uddyotakara’s Nyāyavārttika is a major defensive and constructive work. His task was to re-establish Nyāya against Dignāga’s Buddhist logic and epistemology. The Buddhist challenge was not minor: if perception is strictly nonconceptual, if concepts are constructions, if universals are unreal, and if there is no enduring self, then Nyāya’s entire account of world-directed cognition and stable agency is threatened. Uddyotakara’s response is to strengthen the Nyāya account of perception, language, inference, and selfhood.

On perception, the Nyāya-sūtra defines perception as cognition produced by contact between sense and object, non-deviating and determinate. Later Nyāya elaborates different forms of sense-object contact, including contact with substances, qualities, universals, and relational structures. Uddyotakara emphasizes that perception is produced through such causal connections and that manas-sense and self-manas relations are common conditions for perception.

The crucial anti-Buddhist point is that conceptuality need not falsify perception. For some Buddhist epistemologists, determinate cognition involves conceptual construction and is therefore not pure perception. Nyāya replies that perceiving something as a cow, pot, blue object, or fire can still be perceptual if the cognition is produced by the right causal contact and tracks real features such as universals and qualities. Concepts are not merely subjective overlays; they can disclose real repeatable structures of the world.

This has major consequences for philosophy of mind. Uddyotakara is not merely defending an epistemological definition; he is defending an externalist and realist theory of mental content. A cognition is not individuated only by how it feels from the inside. It is individuated by its object, its causal source, and its success or failure in presenting the object as it is. Mental content is therefore world-involving.

Uddyotakara also reinforces the public, dialectical dimension of mind. Nyāya is not a solitary introspective discipline; it is a theory of inquiry, debate, proof, counter-proof, fallacy, doubt, and defeat. The five-membered argument, the use of examples acceptable to both expert and layperson, and the use of tarka, or suppositional reasoning, show that cognition is embedded in interpersonal rational practice.

5. Udayana: self, agency, and the mature Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika synthesis

Udayana is one of the most important figures in the history of Nyāya. His Ātmatattvaviveka defends the reality of the self, and his Nyāyakusumāñjali gives a major defense of theism. Routledge’s account of Udayana describes him as bringing to a close a long debate between Nyāya and Buddhist philosophers, especially by clarifying how the ātman debate connects with many other areas of philosophical dispute.

Udayana matters for philosophy of mind because he forces the question: What is the subject of experience? Nyāya rejects at least three reductions. The self is not the body, because the body is an object of ownership and use; it is not a sense organ, because the senses are instruments; and it is not a stream of momentary cognitions, because memory, recognition, responsibility, and agency require a persisting subject. The Buddhist alternative, on which causal continuity replaces substantial selfhood, is explicitly one of Nyāya’s major targets.

The argument from memory is especially important. To remember “I saw this before” is not merely to have a present image caused by a past cognition. It involves appropriation of a past experience as one’s own. Nyāya holds that this cannot be explained by a series of momentary cognitions unless one smuggles in a unifying owner of the series. A memory-trace must belong to something, and the subject who now remembers must be connected to the subject who formerly experienced. The self is posited as that enduring locus.

The argument from recognition has similar force. Recognition such as “this is the same person I saw yesterday” or “I experienced this before” binds past and present cognition. Nyāya uses such cases against any view that makes the subject a mere bundle or stream. A stream can contain successive events, but Nyāya asks what makes them one subject’s stream rather than merely a sequence. This question will later reappear in Western debates about personal identity, especially against bundle theories of the self.

Udayana’s mature Nyāya also absorbs the Vaiśeṣika category scheme. In the version inherited by Gaṅgeśa, reality includes substance, quality, motion, universal, individualizer, inherence, and absence. This matters for mind because cognition is analyzed through real relations: a cognition has an object; the object has qualifiers; those qualifiers stand in relations such as inherence; and even absence can be a real object of cognition. The intentional structure of thought is therefore grounded in ontology, not merely in grammar or mental imagery.

Udayana’s theism is not peripheral to the total system, though it is not the same as his philosophy of mind. God is an omniscient, intelligent cause of cosmic order, while individual selves are plural, embodied, finite agents. For mind, this matters because Nyāya’s world is intelligible, ordered, and knowable; cognition is not a mere local accident in a meaningless flux. Udayana’s product-maker argument for God, and his defense of Nyāya theism, reinforce the broader realism within which human cognition operates.

6. Gaṅgeśa: Navya-Nyāya and the technical theory of cognition

Gaṅgeśa’s Tattvacintāmaṇi, often translated as something like “Jewel of Reflection on the Truth,” is organized around the four knowledge-sources: perception, inference, comparison, and testimony. It became a central text for later Nyāya and is traditionally treated as the starting point of Navya-Nyāya. But Gaṅgeśa is best read as a radical refiner of inherited Nyāya, not as someone who discards Old Nyāya.

His most important contribution for philosophy of mind is the precise analysis of cognition’s intentional structure. A cognition is not just a mental glow. It has objecthood, or viṣayatā. It presents something as qualified in some way: a pot as blue, a shell as silver, smoke as pervaded by fire, a prior cognition as one’s own cognition. Later Navya-Nyāya analyzes such cognitions in terms of qualifier, qualificandum, relation, locus, and delimitation. This is a highly technical theory of aboutness centuries before Brentano’s modern reintroduction of intentionality into European philosophy.

Gaṅgeśa’s treatment of perception is especially relevant to contemporary debates about nonconceptual content. He accepts a distinction between indeterminate and determinate perception, but he does not treat indeterminate perception as something plainly available to introspection. It is a theoretical posit: a first-stage awareness that explains how a later determinate cognition can deploy a qualifier or concept. In the standard example, a child’s later recognition of “cow” requires some prior perceptual availability of cowhood or a relevant qualifier.

His account of apperception, anuvyavasāya, is equally important. Apperception is a cognition of a prior cognition or mental event. When I first cognize a pot, I may later cognize “I know the pot” or “I had a pot-cognition.” This second-order cognition is not the original cognition shining by itself; it is a further internally mediated perception. Gaṅgeśa uses this to explain how we know our own cognitions without accepting the Buddhist doctrine that consciousness is intrinsically self-luminous.

This makes Gaṅgeśa close, structurally, to higher-order theories of consciousness, though the comparison must be handled carefully. He is not saying that a mental state becomes conscious only when represented by a higher-order state in exactly the modern sense. Rather, he is saying that a cognition can be taken as object by a later cognition, and that such apperception is crucial for certification, classification, and awareness of one’s own mental life. In illusions, apperception may correctly reveal that one has a silver-cognition without thereby making the silver-cognition true.

Gaṅgeśa also sharpens Nyāya’s externalism about knowledge. Knowledge is factive and produced by a reliable knowledge-source, but the subject need not always possess a fully articulated second-order proof that the source is reliable. Default entitlement can be defeated; doubt and error call for further inquiry. This is why Nyāya combines common-sense realism with sophisticated defeat theory.

His analysis of inference is likewise psychologically relevant. Inference is not merely formal implication. It requires awareness of a reason, the subject under discussion, the property to be proved, and a pervasion relation, vyāpti, such as the relation between smoke and fire. The inferential cognition arises when the subject recognizes that the reason present in the case is pervaded by the probandum. This makes inference a structured cognitive event, not merely an abstract relation among propositions.

7. The comprehensive Nyāya philosophy of mind extracted

The four thinkers together yield a systematic philosophy of mind with the following doctrines.

First, the subject is real, enduring, and non-reducible. Nyāya is a substance theory of self. The self is not identical with body, senses, manas, cognition, memory, or a stream of mental events. It is the locus of cognition, pleasure, pain, desire, aversion, effort, and memory-dispositions. This is the deepest point of continuity from Vātsyāyana through Udayana to Gaṅgeśa.

Second, consciousness is not the essence of the self. This is one of the sharpest differences from many Western and Indian alternatives. Nyāya does not define self as pure consciousness, as Advaita Vedānta often does, nor as a stream of consciousness, as some Buddhist models do, nor as a Cartesian thinking substance whose essence is thought. Cognition is a quality that arises in the self under the right conditions. The self can persist without occurrent cognition.

Third, “mind” is internally differentiated. The English word “mind” hides distinctions that Nyāya insists on keeping separate: ātman as subject, manas as internal organ, buddhi/jñāna as cognition, saṃskāra as disposition, indriya as sense faculty, and śarīra as body. This makes Nyāya more fine-grained than a simple mind/body dualism.

Fourth, cognition is intentional. Every cognition is of or about something. Gaṅgeśa’s later technical vocabulary makes explicit what is already implicit in Old Nyāya: cognition has objecthood, and its truth depends on whether the object is as presented. This gives Nyāya a robust theory of intentionality before intentionality becomes a central theme in modern European philosophy through Brentano.

Fifth, perception is direct but causally mediated. Nyāya is not naïve in the sense of ignoring causal complexity. Perception requires sense-object contact, proper functioning of sense organs, manas, and self. Yet it is realist because perception reaches external objects and their real features. Conceptually structured perception is not automatically downgraded into inference or fiction.

Sixth, attention is explained by manas. Since manas connects the self with particular sensory or internal channels, it explains why ordinary conscious life is sequential. The subject may be capable of many kinds of cognition, but the internal organ functions as a bottleneck or selector. This makes Nyāya’s manas one of the most interesting premodern theories of attention.

Seventh, introspection is not self-luminosity. Nyāya rejects the idea that every cognition reveals itself simply by occurring. Instead, a later cognition can take an earlier cognition as its object. Gaṅgeśa’s anuvyavasāya gives a theory of metacognition: we can know that we know, or know that we have a certain cognition, through a second-order mental perception.

Eighth, error is misqualification, not total mental fabrication. In illusion, such as mistaking a shell for silver, Nyāya does not say that nothing real is involved. There is a real presented substrate, a real or remembered qualifier, and a mistaken relation between them. Error is therefore structurally analyzable: the mind connects a qualifier to the wrong locus. This is a powerful realist alternative to both naïve infallibilism and idealist constructionism.

Ninth, memory presupposes selfhood. Memory is not a pramāṇa in the same sense as perception or inference, because it does not generate new knowledge in the required way. But it is crucial evidence for the self. To remember is not merely to undergo a present image; it is to own a past cognition as one’s own. For Nyāya, this ownership cannot be explained by a bundle of momentary states.

Tenth, affect and motivation are central to mind. Nyāya does not restrict the mental to cognition. Desire, aversion, effort, pleasure, and pain are core marks of the self. The tradition distinguishes cognition from affective states, but it also treats them as causally linked: cognition can generate desire or aversion; desire and aversion generate effort; effort generates action; action perpetuates bondage if guided by ignorance and defect.

Eleventh, philosophy of mind is inseparable from moral psychology. Misconception produces defects; defects produce activity; activity produces karmic continuation and suffering. Correct knowledge, by contrast, dismantles the chain. Nyāya studies mind not only to describe mental life but to end suffering.

Twelfth, liberation is epistemic and affective quieting. Nyāya liberation, apavarga, is classically understood as the cessation of suffering. This is not a romantic theory of eternal aesthetic bliss. It is an austere theory in which the highest good is freedom from the painful cycle of ignorance, defect, action, rebirth, pleasure, and pain.

8. Comparison with Western philosophy of mind

These comparisons are structural, not claims of influence. Nyāya develops in a different intellectual ecology: debate theory, pramāṇa theory, Buddhist challenge, karma, liberation, and Sanskrit theories of language. Still, the overlaps with Western philosophy of mind are substantial.

Aristotle

Aristotle’s psychology is hylomorphic: soul is the form or first actuality of a living body, and psychological states are often treated as involving body as well as soul. Nyāya shares with Aristotle a resistance to crude reductionism and an interest in the embodied conditions of cognition. But the difference is decisive: for Nyāya, the self is not the form of the body. The body is an instrument and site of experience; the self is a distinct enduring substance that can persist beyond a particular body.

Aristotle’s framework makes mind and life deeply continuous; Nyāya makes cognition embodied but does not make selfhood biologically constituted. In modern terms, Aristotle is closer to a non-reductive embodied psychology, while Nyāya is closer to a pluralist substance ontology with embodied causal mediation.

Descartes

Nyāya is often called dualist, but it is not Cartesian dualism. Descartes identifies mind and body as distinct substances characterized by thought and extension. Nyāya also distinguishes self from body, but it does not identify self with continuous thinking. The Nyāya self is not essentially an occurrently conscious substance; consciousness is an episodic quality that arises under causal conditions.

Descartes privileges first-person certainty: the thinking subject discovers itself through the indubitability of thought. Nyāya, especially in its classical form, is less centered on indubitable introspection. It argues for self through the need to ground cognition, desire, memory, pleasure, pain, and agency. Later Nyāya allows sophisticated internal perception and apperception, but even there it rejects the doctrine that cognition is intrinsically self-luminous.

So Nyāya is dualist, but not “mind/body” dualist in the Cartesian sense. It is better described as a self–manas–body/senses model. The English binary “mind versus body” is too crude for it.

Locke

Locke defines a person as a thinking intelligent being capable of reason, reflection, and considering itself as itself across times and places. He ties personal identity to consciousness, memory, and forensic responsibility. Nyāya agrees that memory, appropriation, and responsibility are central, but it reverses the explanatory order: memory does not constitute the self; memory presupposes a persisting self that owns past experience.

This is an important difference. Locke’s problem is often juridical and psychological: what makes a person accountable for past actions? Nyāya’s problem is metaphysical and soteriological: what kind of subject must exist for memory, action, karmic continuity, and liberation to be possible?

Hume and Buddhist no-self

Hume argues that introspection never reveals a substantial self, only particular perceptions, and he is commonly associated with a bundle theory of the self. This is structurally close to the Buddhist challenge faced by Nyāya: if experience consists only of momentary events or aggregates, why posit a substantial owner?

Nyāya’s reply is anti-Humean before Hume: a mere bundle cannot explain ownership, memory, recognition, agency, or the unity of a life. A sequence of perceptions is not yet a subject who remembers, desires, acts, and reaps consequences. Udayana’s defense of ātman is therefore not a mere theological insistence; it is a systematic response to the problem of mental unity.

Brentano and intentionality

Brentano is famous in modern European philosophy for reintroducing intentionality, the aboutness of mental states, into philosophy and psychology. Nyāya, especially in Gaṅgeśa, already has a highly articulated theory of objecthood, viṣayatā. The comparison is legitimate, but the difference is equally important: Nyāya’s intentionality is realist and object-involving. A cognition is not merely directed toward an immanent mental object; it is evaluated by whether it presents an external or otherwise real object as it is.

Gaṅgeśa’s theory also does something that many modern accounts struggle to do: it links intentionality, epistemic warrant, error, conceptual structure, and metaphysical relation in one technical apparatus. A cognition has content because it relates qualifier, qualificandum, and objecthood; it is knowledge if produced by a proper source and true; it is error if the qualifying structure misfires.

Contemporary philosophy of mind

Nyāya has affinities with several contemporary positions, but it is reducible to none of them.

It resembles epistemic externalism because whether a cognition is knowledge depends partly on its causal production by a reliable source, not merely on what is internally accessible to the subject. It resembles direct realism because perception is of external objects rather than private sense-data. It resembles higher-order theories because apperception is a cognition of cognition. It resembles nonconceptual-content debates because of its distinction between indeterminate and determinate perception. But it differs from most contemporary naturalism by retaining an immaterial enduring self, real universals, inherence, karmic continuity, and liberation.

The modern mind-body problem often worries about causal closure: if the physical world is causally closed, how can an immaterial mind affect it? Nyāya does not formulate the problem in modern physicalist terms. It has a broader causal ontology involving substances, qualities, contact, inherence, unseen moral force, and God. That means it avoids some Cartesian formulations of the interaction problem, but a contemporary Nyāya-inspired theory would still need to answer modern causal-closure objections.

9. Major convergences and divergences

The strongest convergences with Western philosophy are these: Nyāya has a mind-body problem, a theory of selfhood, an account of perception, a theory of intentionality, a theory of introspection or metacognition, a theory of memory and personal continuity, and a moral psychology of desire and action. It is plainly part of the global history of philosophy of mind, not merely “logic” or “epistemology” in a narrow sense.

The divergences are equally important. Nyāya does not begin with private consciousness as the foundation of philosophy. It begins with pramāṇa: reliable means of knowing. It does not identify self with thought. It distinguishes self from manas. It does not treat affect as secondary to cognition; desire, aversion, pleasure, pain, and effort are central marks of selfhood. It embeds mind in a soteriological chain from ignorance to suffering and from knowledge to liberation.

A compact comparison looks like this:

Problem Nyāya answer Western analogue Main difference What is the subject? An enduring ātman, distinct from body and cognition Cartesian soul, Lockean person, anti-Humean substance self Nyāya self is not essentially occurrent consciousness. What is mind? Manas is an internal organ; cognition is a quality of self “Mind” as subjective field Nyāya divides what English often fuses. What is perception? Sense-object contact producing veridical cognition when functioning properly Direct realism, Aristotelian perception Nyāya adds manas, pramāṇa theory, and real universals. What is introspection? Apperception: later cognition of prior cognition Higher-order theories, reflection It denies intrinsic self-luminosity. What is error? Misqualification: wrong relation of qualifier to locus Sense-data, representational error, judgment error Nyāya preserves realism even in illusion. What is personal identity? Persistence of self grounds memory and responsibility Locke, Hume, contemporary identity debates Nyāya says memory evidences but does not constitute identity. What is the goal of understanding mind? Liberation from suffering Therapy, ethics, theology, self-knowledge Nyāya gives philosophy of mind an explicitly soteriological telos.

10. Philosophical strengths and tensions

The great strength of the Nyāya account is its refusal to isolate mental phenomena. Cognition is connected to object, source, truth, error, memory, action, debate, and liberation. This gives it a breadth that many modern theories lack. A contemporary philosopher of mind might analyze perception, intentionality, introspection, action, and selfhood in separate subfields; Nyāya treats them as one integrated problem.

Its second strength is the distinction between self, manas, and cognition. This avoids the oversimplified question “Is the mind physical or nonphysical?” Nyāya instead asks: What is the bearer of cognition? What is the instrument of attention? What is the causal route from object to cognition? What makes cognition true? What makes error possible? What makes memory mine? This decomposition is philosophically powerful.

Its third strength is its theory of metacognition. By rejecting self-luminosity while accepting apperception, Nyāya avoids two extremes: it does not say that every cognition is automatically transparent to itself, but it also does not deny that we can know our own mental states. Gaṅgeśa’s account is especially sophisticated because it allows apperception to reveal the intentional character of a cognition without thereby guaranteeing the truth of the first-order cognition.

The main tension, from a modern standpoint, is metaphysical cost. Nyāya requires an immaterial enduring self, real universals, inherence, absence as an ontological category, and often karmic and theistic commitments. These are not incidental; they do real explanatory work. A contemporary appropriation of Nyāya must decide whether to preserve them, reinterpret them, or extract only the analytic structure of its cognition theory.

A second tension concerns liberation. Classical Nyāya’s austere liberation as cessation of suffering can appear psychologically thin if compared with traditions that prize blissful consciousness or divine union. But this austerity is philosophically consistent with Nyāya’s diagnosis: embodied cognition, affect, desire, aversion, pleasure, and pain are bound up with suffering-producing activity. Liberation is not the maximal intensification of experience; it is release from the machinery of painful recurrence.

11. The final extracted model

A comprehensive Nyāya-derived philosophy of mind can be stated as follows:

The mind is not a self-contained inner theater. Mental life is a causal, world-involving, norm-governed process in which a persisting self comes to have cognitions through embodied and internal instruments.

The self is the owner and agent, not the stream. Cognitions occur, but they occur in a subject. Memory, recognition, desire, responsibility, and liberation require that subject.

Cognition is intentional and evaluable. Every cognition presents an object under some qualification. It can be knowledge, doubt, error, memory, or apperception depending on its source, structure, and truth.

Manas is the attentional gate. It connects self to sensory and internal events and explains the seriality of ordinary awareness.

Introspection is a second-order achievement. We know cognitions through further cognition, not because every cognition is intrinsically self-revealing.

Emotion and motivation are not peripheral. Desire, aversion, pleasure, pain, and effort are central mental qualities and are the hinge between cognition and action.

The study of mind is normatively serious. The point is not only to catalogue mental states but to distinguish knowledge from error, wholesome inquiry from confusion, and liberating understanding from suffering-producing misconception.

That is the distinctive Nyāya contribution to global philosophy of mind: a realist, agent-centered, externalist, metacognitive, action-oriented theory of mental life, developed in continuous debate with Buddhist no-self and refined into one of the most exact premodern analyses of cognition and intentionality.

PreviousGautama NextĪśvarakṛṣṇa

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
